one_line_takeaway: An RL-trained questioner agent (GRPO with a validity/incorrectness/diversity reward) automatically discovers a broader, more diverse, and more novel set of ordinary VLM perceptual/reasoning failures on COCO images than static or iterative baselines (47.58-50.73% Failure Discovery Rate vs 39.89% for the best baseline, ConMe) -- this is VLM capability/reliability evaluation research, not a security, adversarial-attack, or red-teaming-for-safety paper.
key_finding: RL-trained questioners substantially outperform all non-RL baselines on both potency and diversity of discovered failures, without a security framing. With Qwen2.5-VL-3B as both questioner and answerer: RL achieves Failure Discovery Rate (FDR) 47.58% and RL+SFT 50.73%, versus the best non-RL baseline ConMe at 39.89% (Zero-Shot 32.20%, SFT 37.02%, Expert Iteration 38.36%). Semantic Diversity (Vendi Score) is 64.96 (RL) / 85.41 (RL+SFT) vs 61.78 (ConMe); Lexical Diversity (prefix entropy) is 0.53/0.57 for RL variants vs 0.30 for ConMe -- nearly double. RL discovers 126 distinct skills (>100-occurrence threshold) versus a baseline plateau of 99-109, including 11 skills exclusive to RL methods (avg failure rate ~50.4%, e.g. hierarchical reasoning 61.2%, damage detection 51.0%) versus 6 skills exclusive to baselines (avg failure rate only ~27.6%). Reward-component ablation (Table 4) shows removing the invalid-question penalty drops QVR from 83.44% to 75.82%; removing Lexical Diversity causes 'reward hacking' where the agent collapses onto narrow question templates (LD falls to as low as 0.16) despite an inflated FDR of 66.70%, which the authors identify as exploiting a narrow set of failure modes rather than genuine broad discovery. Scaling analysis (Table 7) shows increasing answerer capacity lowers FDR as expected, but increasing questioner capacity from 3B to 7B raises FDR by ~9%/21%/32% relative improvement against 3B/7B/8B answerers respectively, with RL widening its advantage over Expert Iteration as answerer capacity grows.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a fully automated Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework for discovering failure modes (i.e., reliability/capability weaknesses, not security vulnerabilities) of a frozen 'candidate' Vision-Language Model (VLM). A VLM-based Questioner agent is trained with GRPO to generate questions about images that elicit incorrect answers from a frozen target VLM (the Answerer), using reward feedback from a separate reasoning VLM (the Verifier). The reward combines question validity (V_Q), incorrectness of the answerer's response (1-C_Q), a diversity term (semantic diversity via cosine distance to a per-image memory bank of prior incorrectly-answered questions, plus lexical diversity via inverse frequency of question prefixes in a global memory bank), and a penalty for invalid questions. The paper also contributes a four-stage pipeline (primitive identification -> BERTopic-based topic modelling -> skill extraction -> meta-skill identification) that automatically builds a hierarchical taxonomy of the cognitive skills probed by discovered failure questions, and a set of new evaluation metrics for the failure-discovery task itself (Question Validity Rate, Failure Discovery Rate, Semantic Diversity via Vendi Score, Lexical Diversity via prefix entropy, Skill Coverage, and Number of Skills).
threat_model: Not applicable -- there is no adversary, attacker, or security threat model in this paper. The 'failure modes' discovered are ordinary perceptual/reasoning mistakes a benign VLM makes when asked genuine, well-formed visual questions about COCO images (e.g., miscounting objects, misidentifying spatial relations, mislocalizing objects). This is model evaluation/robustness-benchmarking research aimed at understanding VLM capability gaps, not an attack, jailbreak, prompt-injection, or adversarial-perturbation study. The authors explicitly distinguish their approach from pixel-level adversarial attacks (citing AttackVLM as prior work they are NOT doing) and from red-teaming of harmful/unsafe outputs -- the Verifier's questioner reward is purely about eliciting factually incorrect visual-question answers, not unsafe/toxic/policy-violating ones.
limitations: The paper's own experiments are confined to a single training/probing image source (1000 images from COCO), and to the Qwen2.5-VL/Qwen3-VL model families (3B-8B parameters) for both questioner and answerer roles -- generalization to other VLM architectures (e.g., GPT-4V, Gemini, LLaVA family) or other image domains beyond COCO is not tested empirically. The core FDR metric depends on an LLM judge (Gemini 3 Flash) whose agreement with human raters is only 75% for FDR and 80% for QVR, meaning roughly a quarter to a fifth of the reported failure/validity judgments would be contested by human annotators. The reward-hacking behavior documented in the ablations (Table 4, Row 2) shows the method is itself fragile to exactly the kind of narrow-template collapse it is designed to avoid unless the lexical-diversity term is included, indicating the multi-term reward requires careful tuning (lambda_scale, lambda_penalty, prefix length L) to avoid degenerate solutions. The paper does not evaluate whether discovered failure modes can be used to actually fix/fine-tune the answerer VLM's underlying weaknesses -- it stops at discovery and taxonomy construction, not remediation.

datasets_used: COCO (Microsoft Common Objects in Context) -- 1000 images randomly sampled from the training split, used as the probing image pool
Existing failure-mode/VQA benchmarks used only to construct SFT training data: BLINK, V*, MMMU-Pro, MMVP, ChartQAPro, OCRBench-v2, core-knowledge-deficits dataset
quantitative_results: Table 1 (Qwen2.5-VL-3B questioner and answerer): FDR -- Zero Shot 32.20%, SFT 37.02%, Expert Iteration 38.36%, ConMe 39.89%, RL 47.58%, RL+SFT 50.73%
Table 1: QVR -- Zero Shot 83.73%, SFT 83.86%, Expert Iteration 88.37%, ConMe 76.63%, RL 83.44%, RL+SFT 86.70%
Table 1: Semantic Diversity (Vendi Score) -- Zero Shot 56.27, SFT 43.80, Expert Iteration 53.42, ConMe 61.78, RL 64.96, RL+SFT 85.41
Table 1: Lexical Diversity -- Zero Shot 0.30, SFT 0.23, Expert Iteration 0.19, ConMe 0.30, RL 0.53, RL+SFT 0.57
Table 1: # Skills discovered -- Zero Shot 109, SFT 99, Expert Iteration 103, ConMe 107, RL 126, RL+SFT 109
Table 2: average per-skill failure rate -- Zero Shot 33.38%, SFT 34.72%, Expert Iteration 38.54%, ConMe 40.36%, RL 48.27%, RL+SFT 48.82%

Table 3: 11 skills exclusive to RL methods, avg failure rate ~50.4% (e.g. hierarchical reasoning 61.2%/53.8%, pattern matching 56.4%/48.6%, damage detection 51.0%/47.9%); 6 skills exclusive to baselines, avg failure rate ~27.6% (e.g. image-caption matching 37.4%, ui recognition 35.9%, cloud detection 16.8%)
Figure 7a: unique skill combinations -- Zero Shot 10784, ConMe 11628, SFT 11678, Expert Iteration 9566, RL 16237, RL+SFT 17940

Table 4 (reward ablation, Qwen2.5-VL-3B): full reward (Sem+Lex+Pen) -> QVR 83.44%, FDR 47.58%, SD 64.96, LD 0.53, #Skills 126; removing invalid penalty -> QVR 75.82%; Lex+Pen only (no Sem) -> QVR 87.18%, FDR 56.54%, SD 68.48, #Skills 118; Pen only (no Sem, no Lex) -> QVR 89.67%, FDR 66.70% (flagged as reward hacking), LD 0.16, #Skills 108
Table 5 (questions-per-image ablation): Qs=1 -> FDR 49.41%, 78 skills; Qs=2 -> FDR 47.58%, 126 skills; Qs=4 -> FDR 42.79%, SD 73.57, 137 skills
Table 6 (prefix-length ablation): L=1 -> LD 0.16, FDR 63.98%; L=2 -> LD 0.53, FDR 47.58%, 126 skills; L=3 -> LD 0.45, SD 82.71; L=4 -> LD 0.32, FDR 53.82%

Table 7 (cross-model scaling, RL method): 3B questioner/3B answerer FDR 47.58%; 3B questioner/7B answerer FDR 44.55%; 3B questioner/8B answerer FDR 29.37%; 7B questioner/3B answerer FDR 51.33%; 7B questioner/7B answerer FDR 54.25%; 7B questioner/8B answerer FDR 38.97% -- relative improvement from 3B->7B questioner of ~9%, ~21%, ~32% for 3B/7B/8B answerers respectively
Human validation: 80% human-Gemini agreement on QVR (2000 samples), 75% agreement on FDR (1200 samples)
Conclusion summary stat: RL discovers 16% more failure-mode skills than the best baseline (126 for RL vs 109 for Zero-Shot)
baselines_compared: ConMe (two-turn in-context prompting method from Huang et al. 2024, reused as-is)
Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on existing failure-mode benchmarks
Expert Iteration (5 rounds of rejection-sampling fine-tuning using the same Verifier as the RL method)
RL+SFT (SFT initialization followed by the paper's RL training)
